meta.purpose: Terminology boundaries, traps, and self-checks for Frontend Motion System.
meta.load_condition: Load with Core for any generation, review, or refactor task in this domain.

standard_terms.definition: A named, version-controlled value that represents a design decision — applied to motion as duration, easing, and distance scales.
A reusable animation function or component that consumes tokens and produces consistent enter, exit, or attention behavior.
A defined range of values for a motion property: e.g., duration-sm (75ms), duration-md (150ms), duration-lg (300ms), duration-xl (500ms).
Naming primitives by user-facing intent (fade-enter, slide-exit, pulse-attention) rather than implementation (opacity-400ms, translateY-20px).

banned_terms.why: Unspecified CSS transitions create inconsistent timing across components. Every transition should reference a motion token or primitive.
banned_terms.replace_with: Replace with specific criteria from the domain ontology.

banned_terms.why: Mockups cannot convey timing, easing, or interaction feedback. Motion decisions must be made in code with live previews, not in static design tools.
banned_terms.replace_with: Replace with specific criteria from the domain ontology.

banned_terms.why: Without a duration scale, 'fine' becomes 200ms in one component and 600ms in another. Define the scale first and pick from it.
banned_terms.replace_with: Replace with specific criteria from the domain ontology.

misunderstandings.wrong: Motion tokens limit creativity and produce boring, uniform animation.
misunderstandings.correct: Tokens define constraints, not outcomes. A limited palette of durations and easings combined with semantic naming produces more creative and coherent results than unlimited one-off values.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Tokens define constraints, not outcomes. A limited palette of durations and easings combined with semantic naming produces more creative and coherent results than unlimited one-off values.
misunderstandings.why: Holding the wrong interpretation leads to poor judgment: Tokens define constraints, not outcomes. A limited palette of durations and easings combined with semantic naming produces more creative and coherent results than unlimited one-off values.
applies_when: Designing a shared design system where multiple teams implement hover, focus, and loading states.
Building a multi-page application requiring rhythmic consistency across route transitions and micro-interactions.
Onboarding new developers to a motion-heavy codebase where standardized easing curves reduce cognitive load.
does_not_apply_when: Prototyping an experimental marketing landing page where breaking motion conventions is a deliberate artistic goal.
Debugging a performance bottleneck in a legacy browser that requires a hardcoded, highly optimized duration override.
Implementing a one-off, complex physics-based animation that tightly couples to unique DOM layout quirks.
misunderstandings.failure_risk: The agent will enforce rigid token usage across all animations, rejecting necessary custom timing values and producing stiff, emotionally flat interactions that fail to meet specific UX or artistic requirements.

misunderstandings.wrong: Copying animation code between components is fine for consistency.
misunderstandings.correct: Copy-pasted code diverges over time and becomes a maintenance burden. Extract the behavior into a named primitive that stays in sync.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Copy-pasted code diverges over time and becomes a maintenance burden. Extract the behavior into a named primitive that stays in sync.
misunderstandings.why: Holding the wrong interpretation leads to poor judgment: Copy-pasted code diverges over time and becomes a maintenance burden. Extract the behavior into a named primitive that stays in sync.
applies_when: Reusing a fade-in or slide-up pattern across multiple modal, card, and list components in a growing codebase.
Updating a shared easing curve or duration value application-wide after a UX design review.
Scaling a product with dozens of interactive elements that require synchronized motion behavior across independent feature teams.
does_not_apply_when: Writing a highly unique, component-specific animation that is tightly coupled to a complex DOM structure and will never be reused.
Quickly prototyping a throwaway demo or hackathon project where the overhead of abstraction outweighs the benefit.
Temporarily inlining animation configuration to isolate and debug a flaky state-driven transition.
misunderstandings.failure_risk: The agent will aggressively refactor every inline animation into abstracted primitives, introducing unnecessary indirection and over-engineered utilities that complicate debugging for truly one-off interactions.

self_check: Are duration, easing, and distance tokens defined and version-controlled?
Do shared motion primitives cover the most common animation patterns in the product?
Can designers preview every motion primitive without running the full application?
Does CI enforce that new animations use primitives or tokens rather than raw values?
